description:
You are acting as a monitoring specialist who is standardising alert “seed” files for the container-orchestration team.  
Create a brand-new JSON definition file that will later be picked up by the in-house alert-loader.

Desired end-state:

1. Directory:
   • /home/user/alerts  
     (Create it if it does **not** already exist.)

2. File:
   • /home/user/alerts/container_cpu_alert.json

3. Exact file contents  
   (including line breaks, spacing and final trailing newline; automated grading is strict and will do a byte-for-byte comparison):

{
  "alert": "container_cpu_usage",
  "threshold_percent": 80,
  "action": "email",
  "recipients": [
    "ops-team@example.com"
  ]
}

4. Creation method:
   • Accomplish everything (directory creation *and* file write) with **one** shell command that you type in the terminal.

5. Validation:
   • After running your single command, you may run supplementary commands (e.g. cat, ls) to show the file exists and its contents are correct, but the grading script only checks that the file exists at the exact path above and that its contents match the JSON block verbatim.
truth:
Expected artefacts after successful execution:

1. Directory:
   Path: /home/user/alerts  
   Permissions: default user-created directory (drwxr-xr-x or stricter).

2. File:
   Path: /home/user/alerts/container_cpu_alert.json  
   Permissions: default user-created file (-rw-r--r-- or stricter).  
   Content (exact, including the final newline character at EOF):

{
  "alert": "container_cpu_usage",
  "threshold_percent": 80,
  "action": "email",
  "recipients": [
    "ops-team@example.com"
  ]
}

Automated tests will:
• Confirm that /home/user/alerts/container_cpu_alert.json exists.  
• Read the file and compare its SHA-256 hash to that of the JSON block above (including the newline).  
• Fail if any extra whitespace, missing newline, or different characters are present.
name: task_000000_e2f2a65e
